questions.question: Compared with adults, children have:
options: Proportionally larger heads and smaller airways
Smaller body surface area relative to mass
questions.rationale: Children have proportionally larger heads, smaller/more easily obstructed airways, flexible ribs, and larger relative BSA.

questions.question: The earliest sign of shock in a child is:
questions.rationale: Children compensate with tachycardia and vasoconstriction; hypotension appears late.

questions.question: Hypotension in an injured child indicates:
options: A late, pre-arrest sign of decompensation
questions.rationale: Because children compensate so well, hypotension is a late and ominous sign of impending collapse.

questions.question: The most common path to pediatric cardiac arrest is:
questions.rationale: Respiratory failure leading to hypoxia is the usual route to pediatric arrest—support breathing early.

questions.question: To maintain neutral airway/spinal alignment in a small child, pad:
questions.rationale: The large occiput flexes the neck on a flat surface; padding under the torso restores neutral position.

questions.question: A length-based resuscitation tape provides:
options: Estimated weight, drug doses, fluid volumes, and equipment sizes
questions.rationale: The tape estimates weight and supplies matched doses, volumes, and equipment sizes.

questions.question: A typical pediatric fluid bolus for decompensated shock is:
questions.rationale: About 20 mL/kg of warmed isotonic crystalloid is given and reassessed/repeated per protocol.

questions.question: SCIWORA refers to:
options: Spinal Cord Injury Without Radiographic Abnormality
questions.rationale: A child's elastic spine can injure the cord without a visible fracture—maintain suspicion based on mechanism.

questions.question: Why can children sustain a pulmonary contusion without rib fractures?
options: Their flexible ribs transmit force to the lung without breaking
Their lungs are protected by fat
questions.rationale: Compliant pediatric ribs bend and transmit energy to the lung, causing contusion without fracture.

questions.question: Children lose heat rapidly because of their:
options: Large body surface area relative to mass
questions.rationale: A high surface-area-to-mass ratio promotes rapid heat loss; keep injured children warm.

questions.question: For a critically injured child without a parent present, you should:
options: Wait for consent before any care
Provide immediate life-saving care under implied consent
questions.rationale: Emergency (implied) consent allows immediate life-saving treatment regardless of parental availability.

questions.question: Which finding suggests non-accidental trauma (abuse)?
options: A single bruise from a witnessed fall
Injuries inconsistent with the history or in different healing stages
questions.rationale: Inconsistent histories, patterned injuries, and injuries in different healing stages are red flags for abuse.

questions.question: EMS providers regarding suspected child abuse are:
options: Mandated reporters who document and report per protocol
questions.rationale: EMS providers are mandated reporters; document objectively and report per protocol.

questions.question: When peripheral IV access fails in a critical child, the alternative is:
questions.rationale: IO access provides rapid, reliable delivery when peripheral access fails.

questions.question: A normal blood pressure in a tachycardic, injured child should be interpreted as:
options: Compensated shock that can suddenly decompensate
questions.rationale: Normal pressure with tachycardia reflects compensation; deterioration can be abrupt—treat and monitor closely.

questions.question: Multisystem injury is common in children because:
options: Their organs are close together and less protected
questions.rationale: Closely packed, less-protected organs mean a single force often injures multiple systems.

questions.question: Building rapport with an injured child is best done by:
options: Getting to eye level, staying calm, and keeping a caregiver near when possible
Separating the child from all family
questions.rationale: A calm, eye-level, honest approach with a caregiver nearby reduces fear and improves cooperation.

questions.question: The leading principle of pediatric resuscitation equipment selection is to:
options: Use a length-based tape or pediatric reference for correct sizing
questions.rationale: A length-based tape ensures correctly sized airway, vascular, and immobilization equipment.
